section.title: We Have Sinned Like Our Fathers
section.sectionRationale: The closing psalm of Book IV, Psalms 90 to 106, and the deliberate companion of Psalm 105: where that psalm rehearsed God's covenant faithfulness, this one confesses Israel's repeated rebellion across the very same history, from the Red Sea to the wilderness to the land of Canaan. It ends with a doxology that closes the whole book, and with a plea to be gathered from among the nations, a prayer answered in part at the return from exile and finally in Christ's gathering of His scattered people.
section.thesis: Israel's history, from the Red Sea through the golden calf, the refusal of the promised land, Baal of Peor, and generations of idolatry in Canaan, is one long record of forgetting God's works, yet at every turn His steadfast love, not Israel's worthiness, is what saves, relents, and finally gathers His people home.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: a call to praise and a personal plea, verses 1 to 5; rebellion and rescue at the Red Sea, verses 6 to 12; craving and envy in the wilderness, verses 13 to 18; the golden calf and Moses' intercession, verses 19 to 23; refusing the promised land, verses 24 to 27; Baal of Peor and the zeal of Phinehas, verses 28 to 31; Meribah and the failure to drive out the nations, verses 32 to 39; and judgment, mercy, and a closing plea to gather Israel from the nations, verses 40 to 48.

units.label: Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good
units.note: a call to praise and a personal plea

verses.text: Hallelujah! Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good; His {{chesed|loving devotion}} endures forever.
Who can describe the mighty acts of the LORD or fully proclaim His praise?
Blessed are those who uphold justice, who practice righteousness at all times.
Remember me, O LORD, in Your favor to Your people; visit me with Your salvation,
that I may see the prosperity of Your chosen ones, and rejoice in the gladness of Your nation, and give glory with Your inheritance.

units.label: We have sinned like our fathers
units.note: rebellion and rescue at the Red Sea

verses.text: We have sinned like our fathers; we have done wrong and acted wickedly.
Our fathers in Egypt did not grasp Your wonders or remember Your abundant kindness; but they rebelled by the sea, there at the Red Sea.
Yet He saved them for the sake of His name, to make His power known.
He rebuked the Red Sea, and it dried up; He led them through the depths as through a desert.
He saved them from the hand that hated them; He redeemed them from the hand of the enemy.
The waters covered their foes; not one of them remained.
Then they believed His promises and sang His praise.

units.label: They soon forgot His works
units.note: craving and envy in the wilderness

verses.text: Yet they soon forgot His works and failed to wait for His counsel.
They craved intensely in the wilderness and tested God in the desert.
So He granted their request, but sent a wasting disease upon them.
In the camp they envied Moses, as well as Aaron, the holy one of the LORD.
The earth opened up and swallowed Dathan; it covered the assembly of Abiram.
Then fire blazed through their company; flames consumed the wicked.

units.label: They exchanged their Glory
units.note: the golden calf and Moses' intercession

verses.text: At Horeb they made a calf and worshiped a molten image.
They exchanged their Glory for the image of a grass-eating ox.
They forgot God their Savior, who did great things in Egypt,
wondrous works in the land of Ham, and awesome deeds by the Red Sea.
So He said He would destroy them, had not Moses His chosen one stood before Him in the {{peretz|breach}} to divert His wrath from destroying them.
They despised the pleasant land; they did not believe His promise.
They grumbled in their tents and did not listen to the voice of the LORD.
So He raised His hand and swore to cast them down in the wilderness,
to disperse their offspring among the nations and scatter them throughout the lands.

units.label: Phinehas stood and intervened
units.note: Baal of Peor and the zeal of Phinehas

verses.text: They yoked themselves to Baal of Peor and ate sacrifices offered to lifeless gods.
So they provoked the LORD to anger with their deeds, and a plague broke out among them.
But Phinehas stood and intervened, and the plague was restrained.
It was {{chashab|credited}} to him as righteousness for endless generations to come.

units.label: They mingled with the nations
units.note: Meribah and the failure to drive out the nations

verses.text: At the waters of Meribah they angered the LORD, and trouble came to Moses because of them.
For they rebelled against His Spirit, and Moses spoke rashly with his lips.
They did not destroy the peoples as the LORD had commanded them,
but they mingled with the nations and adopted their customs.
They worshiped their idols, which became a snare to them.
They sacrificed their sons and their daughters to demons.
They shed innocent blood, the blood of their sons and daughters, whom they sacrificed to the idols of Canaan, and the land was polluted with blood.
They defiled themselves by their actions and prostituted themselves by their deeds.

units.label: Save us, O LORD our God
units.note: judgment, mercy, and a closing plea to gather Israel

verses.text: So the anger of the LORD burned against His people, and He abhorred His own inheritance.
He delivered them into the hand of the nations, and those who hated them ruled over them.
Their enemies oppressed them and subdued them under their hand.
Many times He rescued them, but they were bent on rebellion and sank down in their iniquity.
Nevertheless He heard their cry; He took note of their distress.
And He remembered His covenant with them, and relented by the abundance of His loving devotion.
He made them objects of compassion to all who held them captive.
Save us, O LORD our God, and gather us from the nations, that we may give thanks to Your holy name, that we may glory in Your praise.
Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Let all the people say, "Amen!" Hallelujah!

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, loyal covenant kindness. This word frames the whole psalm, verses 1 and 45, the one constant across a story otherwise marked by human failure.

peretz.gloss: A breach, a gap in a wall or defense. Moses stood in this gap between God's wrath and the people, verse 23, a picture of intercession that the New Testament applies to Christ's own mediating work.

chashab.gloss: To credit, reckon, or count as. The same verb used of Abraham's faith being credited as righteousness, Genesis 15:6; here Phinehas' zealous act is credited to him in the same way, tying this psalm's language directly to the doctrine of righteousness reckoned by grace.

basic.context: This psalm closes Book IV of the Psalter with a national confession: we have sinned like our fathers. It retells the same history as Psalm 105, the Red Sea, the wilderness, the golden calf, Canaan, but from the opposite angle, cataloging Israel's repeated rebellion against a God who kept saving them anyway.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm is corporate confession set inside praise, opening and closing with Hallelujah even though the middle is almost entirely a record of failure. It is meant to be prayed by later generations as their own confession, we have sinned like our fathers, not merely read as ancient history.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm is bracketed by chesed, God's loving devotion, verses 1 and 45, the one constant thread. Moses stood in the peretz, the breach, verse 23, between God's wrath and the people. Phinehas' zeal was chashab, credited, to him as righteousness, verse 31, the same verb used of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6.
basic.typology: Moses standing in the breach to turn away God's wrath, verse 23, prefigures Christ, the greater Mediator who always lives to intercede, Hebrews 7:25. Phinehas' zeal, credited to him as righteousness, verse 31, echoes the pattern of righteousness reckoned by grace that culminates in the gospel, Romans 4:3-5.
basic.christ: Moses' intercession in the breach, verse 23, anticipates Christ, our great High Priest who does not merely delay God's wrath for a moment but bears it fully and lives forever to intercede for His people, Hebrews 7:25, 1 Timothy 2:5. The psalm's closing plea to be gathered from the nations, verse 47, is answered ultimately in Christ's gathering of His scattered sheep into one flock, John 11:52.
basic.god: God is shown as righteously angered by persistent unbelief and idolatry, yet His anger never has the last word; verse after verse of failure is met by rescue, and the psalm's own refrain insists it is for His name's sake and because of His steadfast love, not Israel's merit, that He acts.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 32 records the golden calf in full. Numbers 25 records Baal of Peor and Phinehas' zeal. Hebrews 7:25 presents Christ as the Mediator who always lives to intercede, fulfilling what Moses pictured only partially. Romans 4:3-5 explains righteousness credited by faith, the same word used of Phinehas here.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's long record of Israel forgetting God's works, met again and again by His steadfast love rather than by what they deserved, is the very shape of the gospel, and it finds its resolution in Christ, the Mediator who stands in the ultimate breach and the Shepherd who gathers God's scattered people home for good.

context.literary: A national confession structured as seven scenes of rebellion, each following the same pattern, sin, consequence, and God's undeserved rescue or restraint, framed by an opening call to praise and personal plea, verses 1 to 5, and a closing plea for gathering and praise, verses 47 to 48.
context.historical: Likely used, or composed, in the exilic or post-exilic period, since verse 47 voices the hope of a scattered people longing to be gathered again; it functions as a corporate mirror, inviting later generations to own the same sins and seek the same undeserved mercy.
context.canonical: The deliberate companion to Psalm 105, and the closing psalm of Book IV; its final verse, Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting, Let all the people say, Amen, Hallelujah, functions as the doxology closing the whole book, echoed in the doxologies that end Books I through III.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead later generations of God's people into honest, corporate confession, we have sinned like our fathers, by rehearsing a long history of rebellion, and to root all hope for restoration not in Israel's improved behavior but in God's remembered covenant and abundant loving devotion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes specific historical rebellions, the Red Sea, the golden calf, Baal of Peor, and more; it prescribes as an abiding pattern honest corporate confession of sin and continual dependence on God's mercy rather than self-trust.

debates.question: How should Moses' intercession in the breach, verse 23, be understood in relation to Christ's mediation?

views.label: Moses functions as a historical leader whose plea providentially satisfied for that moment
views.case: The text presents a specific, bounded historical event, God's decree to destroy and Moses' successful plea recorded in Exodus 32:11-14; it need not be pressed into a full doctrine of atonement.

views.label: Moses is a deliberate type of the greater Mediator
views.case: Moses standing in the breach to turn away wrath anticipates a pattern the New Testament explicitly builds on, presenting Christ as the final Mediator who does not merely delay wrath but bears it fully, Hebrews 7:25, 1 Timothy 2:5.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Moses' intercession was real and effective within its historical moment, and the New Testament deliberately develops this very pattern to present Christ as the sufficient and permanent Mediator, so the two readings complement rather than compete.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes on chesed, God's steadfast, loyal love, verses 1 and 45, the one constant across a story of repeated failure. Moses stood in the peretz, the breach, verse 23, between God's wrath and the people, and Phinehas' zealous act was chashab, credited, to him as righteousness, verse 31, the same verb used of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6.

typology.type: Moses standing in the breach to turn away wrath, verse 23
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures Christ, the great High Priest who always lives to intercede for His people, Hebrews 7:25, 1 Timothy 2:5.

typology.type: Phinehas' zeal credited to him as righteousness, verse 31
typology.fulfillment: Echoes the pattern of righteousness reckoned by grace through faith that culminates in the gospel, Romans 4:3-5, Genesis 15:6.

study.christ: Moses' intercession in the breach, verse 23, anticipates Christ, our great High Priest who does not merely delay God's wrath for a moment but bears it fully at the cross and lives forever to intercede for His people, Hebrews 7:25, 1 Timothy 2:5. The psalm's closing plea, save us and gather us from the nations, verse 47, is answered ultimately in Christ's gathering of His scattered sheep into one flock, John 11:52, John 10:16.
study.god: God is righteously angered by persistent unbelief and idolatry, His wrath burning against covenant-breaking, verse 40, yet His anger never has the last word in this psalm; failure after failure is met by rescue, restraint, or relenting mercy, and the psalm insists this is for His name's sake and because of His abundant loving devotion, not because Israel earned it.

crossReferences.note: Records the golden calf and Moses' intercession in full, the event summarized in verses 19-23 of this psalm.
Records Baal of Peor and Phinehas' zeal, summarized in verses 28-31 of this psalm.
Presents Christ as the Mediator who always lives to intercede, fulfilling what Moses' standing in the breach only pictured.
Explains righteousness credited by faith apart from works, using the same language applied to Phinehas in verse 31.

study.oneStory: This psalm's long record of Israel forgetting God's works, met again and again by His steadfast love rather than by what they deserved, is the very shape of the gospel, and it finds its resolution in Christ, the Mediator who stands in the ultimate breach and the Shepherd who gathers God's scattered people home for good.

sermon.title: A Pastor's Role in World Missions
sermon.why: Piper anchors this message in Psalm 106:7-8, Yet He saved them for the sake of His name, to make His power known, tracing that same principle, God acting for His own glory rather than human worthiness, through the whole sweep of redemptive history; it directly illuminates this psalm's central turn, that mercy triumphed over Israel's rebellion because of God's name, not their merit.

quiz.q: According to verses 7-8, why did God save Israel at the Red Sea despite their rebellion there?

options.text: For the sake of His own name, to make His power known.
options.note: The psalm is explicit that God's motive was His own name and power, not Israel's worthiness, since they had just rebelled.

options.text: Because Israel had repented thoroughly before crossing the sea.
options.note: The text says they rebelled at the sea; the rescue is attributed to God's name, not prior repentance.

options.text: Because Moses bribed God with a sacrifice.
options.note: No such bribery is described; the psalm credits God's own name and power as the reason for salvation.

options.text: Because the Egyptians surrendered without a fight.
options.note: The Egyptians pursued and were destroyed in the sea, verse 11; they did not surrender.

quiz.q: What did Israel do at Horeb, according to verses 19-20?

options.text: They made a calf and worshiped a molten image, exchanging their Glory for the image of a grass-eating ox.
options.note: The golden calf incident, Exodus 32, is the clearest instance in the psalm of Israel trading the living God for a lifeless idol.

options.text: They built the tabernacle exactly as God commanded.
options.note: The tabernacle's faithful construction is not in view here; this passage describes the golden calf, an act of rebellion, not obedience.

options.text: They fasted and prayed for forty days.
options.note: Moses fasted on the mountain, but the people below were making and worshiping the calf, the opposite of faithful prayer.

options.text: They asked Aaron to intercede for their sins.
options.note: Aaron in fact made the calf for them; it was Moses, not a request through Aaron, who later interceded, verse 23.

quiz.q: Who stood in the breach to turn away God's wrath after the golden calf, verse 23?

options.text: Moses, God's chosen one.
options.note: Moses' intercession, recorded fully in Exodus 32:11-14, prevented God's decreed destruction of the people.

options.text: Aaron, the high priest.
options.note: Aaron had made the calf; it was Moses who interceded to turn away God's wrath.

options.text: Phinehas, the priest.
options.note: Phinehas' zeal comes later in the psalm, at Baal of Peor, verses 28-31, not at the golden calf.

options.text: Joshua, Moses' assistant.
options.note: The text specifically names Moses as the one who stood in the breach, not Joshua.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say happened when Phinehas intervened at Baal of Peor, verses 30-31?

options.text: The plague was restrained, and it was credited to him as righteousness for endless generations.
options.note: This directly echoes the language of Genesis 15:6, righteousness credited by faith, applied here to Phinehas' zealous act.

options.text: The plague grew worse and spread to neighboring nations.
options.note: The text says the plague was restrained by his intervention, not that it worsened.

options.text: He was punished for taking matters into his own hands.
options.note: The text credits his action as righteousness, the opposite of punishment.

options.text: He was made king over Israel as a reward.
options.note: The reward named in the text is that his act was credited as righteousness, not a royal appointment.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing plea, verse 47?

options.text: Save us, O LORD our God, and gather us from the nations, that we may give thanks to Your holy name.
options.note: The confession of repeated failure ends not in despair but in a plea for salvation and gathering, resting on God's character rather than Israel's record.

options.text: Give us a king who will never sin.
options.note: No such request appears; the plea is for God's salvation and gathering, not for a sinless human king.

options.text: Let us remain scattered so we may learn humility.
options.note: The plea is the opposite, to be gathered from the nations, not to remain scattered.

options.text: Grant us victory over all our earthly enemies.
options.note: The specific request named is salvation and gathering for the purpose of praise, not simply military victory.

questions: The psalm opens with the confession, we have sinned like our fathers, verse 6. What patterns of sin do you see repeating in your own life that echo failures you have seen in others before you?
Verse 13 says Israel soon forgot His works after the Red Sea. What recent evidence of God's faithfulness in your life are you already at risk of forgetting?

God saved Israel for the sake of His own name, verse 8, not because they deserved it. How does resting your assurance on God's character rather than your own performance change the way you pray today?

Moses stood in the breach to turn away wrath, verse 23, a picture fulfilled in Christ's ongoing intercession for us, Hebrews 7:25. How does knowing Christ is praying for you right now change how you face your own failure?

The psalm ends with a plea to be gathered and to give thanks, verse 47, even after cataloging so much failure. What would it look like for you to move from shame over past sin to an honest plea for God's ongoing mercy?

Reflect on the whole sweep of this psalm: God rescues, Israel forgets, God rescues again. Where in your life do you need to trust that God's steadfast love, not your consistency, is what holds your relationship with Him together?

translationNotes.note: The Red Sea can also be rendered the Sea of Reeds, reflecting uncertainty about the precise body of water; the same alternate rendering applies in verses 9 and 22.
Their Glory, referring to God Himself, can also be rendered their glorious God; either way the verse describes the tragedy of exchanging the living God for a lifeless image.
Meribah means quarreling in Hebrew, naming the place after Israel's conflict with God there, compare Exodus 17:7.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 106, since the local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error. Footnote markers [a] through [g] were stripped; no em or en dashes appear in the source verses for this psalm. Verse count (48) and first/last verse wording cross-checked against PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md bounds, Psalm 106:1-106:48. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'A Pastor's Role in World Missions' at desiringgod.org, which is indexed under Scripture Psalm 106 and is anchored in Psalm 106:7-8.
